coredata.dc:title: Experimental characterization of a hybrid impinging microjet-microchannel heat sink fabricated using high-volume metal additive manufacturing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Kempers, R.
Colenbrander, J.
Tan, W.
Chen, R.
Robinson, A.J.
coredata.dc:description: A high-performance water-cooled micro heat sink for the thermal management of high heat flux microelectronics was designed, fabricated, and tested. The heat sink design leverages a multi- metal electrodeposition additive manufacturing process to produce complex flow which are impossible to fabricate with traditional processes. A previous study employed simulation-driven design to develop and optimize micro heat sinks and is a hybrid of microchannels with an array of integrated microjets. In the present investigation, a prototype heat sink was fabricated and tested at heat fluxes up to and exceeding 1000 W/cm2. The results demonstrate reasonable agreement between the numerical predictions and experimental results, considering the complex geometry flow and conjugate heat transfer within the device. From a thermal–hydraulic performance standpoint, the heat exchanger achieved an estimated overall thermal conductance of ~330 kW/m2K with a pressure drop of 160 kPa (23 psi) for a flow rate of 0.5 L/min. For inlet water at 20 °C, this corresponded to a measured base temperature of 54 °C at an applied heat flux of 1000 W/cm2. The hybrid microchannel–microjet heat sink further exhibited an enhancement ratio of 6 when compared with a microchannel heat exchanger of commensurate design. To the extent of our knowledge, this microfluidic heat exchanger has achieved one of the highest effective thermal conductance levels reported in the literature and has done so at moderate pressure drop and flow rate.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Micro heat sink
Micro heat exchanger
Micro cold plate
High volume micro additive manufacturing and 3d printing
High heat flux
Microjets and microchannels

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design and manufacturing of additively manufactured heat sinks
Hybrid impinging micro-jet array microchannel heat sink (FINJET)

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2020 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cross-sectional view of a unit cell.
Section view of the FINJET heat sink.
Section view of baseline microchannel heat sink.
FINJET integrated to a header (header cross-section shown).
a) Schematic of experimental flow loop, b) Photograph of assembled test section showing heater block and underside of heat exchanger and header.
Diagram of the heater block and heat sink assembly.
a) optical microscope images of the FINJET heat sink, b) close-up of jet nozzle orifices.
Comparison of experimental and simulated FINJET heat sink performance [27] for an imposed heat flux of 1000 W/cm2.
Comparison of experimental and simulated FINJET heat sink performance [27] for an imposed heat flux of 1000 W/cm2.
Postmortem SEM image of FINJET inlet surface showing debris from header soldering process blocking jet nozzle orifices.
Effective R-value and thermal resistance of the FINJET heat sink with varying flow rate.
Coefficient of performance of the FINJET heat sink with varying flow rate.
Effective thermal conductance comparison between the FINJET and baseline microchannel heat sink.
Pressure drop and pumping power comparison between microjet and microchannel heat sinks.
Ratio of thermal conductance to pressure drop for microjet and microchannel heat sinks.

head.ce:title: Experimental characterization of a hybrid impinging microjet-microchannel heat sink fabricated using high-volume metal additive manufacturing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Confluent Research Ltd., 6 The Avenue, Inse Bay, Laytown, Ireland
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bConfluent Research Ltd., 6 The Avenue, Inse Bay, Laytown, Ireland

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Microfabrica, 7911 Haskell Ave, Van Nuys, California
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cMicrofabrica, 7911 Haskell Ave, Van Nuys, California

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A high-performance water-cooled micro heat sink for the thermal management of high heat flux microelectronics was designed, fabricated, and tested. The heat sink design leverages a multi- metal electrodeposition additive manufacturing process to produce complex flow which are impossible to fabricate with traditional processes. A previous study employed simulation-driven design to develop and optimize micro heat sinks and is a hybrid of microchannels with an array of integrated microjets.

In the present investigation, a prototype heat sink was fabricated and tested at heat fluxes up to and exceeding 1000 W/cm2. The results demonstrate reasonable agreement between the numerical predictions and experimental results, considering the complex geometry flow and conjugate heat transfer within the device.

From a thermal–hydraulic performance standpoint, the heat exchanger achieved an estimated overall thermal conductance of ~330 kW/m2K with a pressure drop of 160 kPa (23 psi) for a flow rate of 0.5 L/min. For inlet water at 20 °C, this corresponded to a measured base temperature of 54 °C at an applied heat flux of 1000 W/cm2. The hybrid microchannel–microjet heat sink further exhibited an enhancement ratio of 6 when compared with a microchannel heat exchanger of commensurate design. To the extent of our knowledge, this microfluidic heat exchanger has achieved one of the highest effective thermal conductance levels reported in the literature and has done so at moderate pressure drop and flow rate.

ce:keyword.ce:text: High volume micro additive manufacturing and 3d printing

ce:section.ce:para: Electronic components are continually becoming more powerful and smaller in size, which is driving peak and average heat fluxes to levels beyond the point where boardlevel air cooling is a viable option [1]. For example, commercial RF power amplifiers exist today at sub-centimeter dimensions while producing heat fluxes in the range of 1000 W/cm2. The thermal problem becomes exacerbated when electronic packages are highly integrated, because there is limited real estate available for thermal management hardware at board level. It is now recognized that board level liquid cooling is the solution to these key issues because it can offer far superior heat transfer in compact form factors. Interestingly, reducing heat transfer feature size and characteristic dimensions has a positive influence on the heat transfer effectiveness, and this was recognized over thirty years ago in the pivotal work of Tuckermann and Pease [2]. Generally considered the beginning of microfluidic heat exchanger science, Tuckermann and Pease [2] described the phenomenon whereby the heat transfer coefficient increases with the inverse of the channel hydraulic diameter and they developed and tested a single phase microchannel heat exchanger with dimensions in the order of 100 s of micrometers, showing unprecedented effective thermal conductance. Notably, the high convective heat transfer coefficients resulting from the sub-millimeter channel size were reinforced by a high surface area per unit volume, with the walls separating the channels now effectively acting as fins. In this sense, micro-featured heat exchangers take advantage of the high convective heat transfer coefficient, while simultaneously providing substantial surface area per unit volume; together, this results in effective thermal conductance and form factors which are difficult to achieve in larger scale systems. Thus, it can be said that there is a positive tension between reducing electronic component size and level of integration because the heat transfer hardware can be designed in such a way that the thermal performance improves with decreasing size. However, there also exists a negative tension in the context of hydraulic demands with decreasing length scale, because the pressure drop escalates with decreasing channel size. The pressure drops associated with micro heat exchangers puts severe limits on the viable flow rates and has a knock-on effect in terms of the sensible heating of the liquid, in that the liquid temperature rise of the coolant is inversely proportional to the flow rate. Excessive temperature rise of the working fluid will cause non-uniformity in the temperature of the underlying chip which is undesirable due to thermal stresses induced by differential thermal expansions of the constituent materials of the component. Thus, the design of single-phase micro heat exchangers must involve full consideration of the thermal–hydraulic performance of the overall system, in which as high as feasible effective thermal conductance is achieved with as low as possible pressure drop and temperature non-uniformity.

Since the work of Tuckermann and Pease [2], a vast range of microfluidic heat exchangers have been conceived, most often using computational fluid dynamics (CFD) as the main simulation design tool. The primary goal of the various designs was to invent and verify techniques of enhancing heat transfer without excessive hydraulic penalty and, to a much lesser extent, temperature uniformity. The concepts range from simple design optimization of channel cross-sections [3,4], flow obstructions [5,6,7,8,9], wavy and zig-zag channels [10,11,12], and periodic pinching of the channels and channel roughness [13,14,15]; these all aimed to increase mixing and heat transfer area within the channels. Complex 3D conceptual designs [16,17,18] have also been investigated; these partly address the problems associated with high imposed heat fluxes causing large surface temperature gradients. This is not an exhaustive list of designs conceived to enhance the thermal hydraulics of microfluidic heat exchangers; rather it highlights that there are several design options available for engineering such technologies. In terms of thermal–hydraulic performance of through-channel type heat exchangers, offset strip fins tend to outperform other concepts in terms of achieving exceptionally highly effective thermal conductance with practical pressure drops. As examples, [19] and [20] indicate that thermal conductance in the range of 125–200 kW/m2K are achievable at a moderate (<100 kPa) pressure drop.

The above discussion focusses on through-channel type heat exchangers. Here, the achievable convective component of the overall heat transfer coefficients is of the order of magnitude of ~10 kW/m2K with water as the coolant. Achieving in the order of ~100 kW/m2K overall thermal conductance thus requires extended surface area. Alternatively, liquid microjet array impingement heat transfer can achieve surface average convective heat transfer coefficients of this order of magnitude without requiring surface area enhancement. Albeit receiving much less attention in the open literature, microjets are proving to be a competing technology for high heat flux cooling of electronics because they produce significantly higher convective heat transfer coefficients than their channel-type counterparts. For example, Michna et al. [21] demonstrated microjet array convective heat transfer coefficients of circa 200–300 kW/m2K for pressure drops in the region of 25–100 kPa, albeit for a 1 mm2 target heated area. These rather extreme convective heat transfer coefficients are largely due to the high jet velocities created by microjets which then have a commensurately high stagnation heat transfer coefficient. Between neighboring jets, the heat transfer deteriorates rapidly, such that a key design challenge for microjet array cooling is achieving high cooling effectiveness over large surface areas [22]. It is also worth noting that, since fresh coolant is delivered over the entire heated impingement area, microjet array cooling should result in improved temperature uniformity compared with through-channel solutions, though this is rarely quantified.

Considering the discussion above, it is not surprising that some researchers have considered hybrid micro heat exchanger designs, with the aim of incorporating the benefits of the highly convective heat transfer coefficients associated with microjets and the enhanced surface area of microchannels. Examples include [23,24], where lines of microjets are injected into linear microchannels. Han et al. [23], for example, achieved over 115 kW/m2K effective thermal conductance with an impressively low pressure drop of 30 kPa. One key aspect that compromises performance of these hybrid heat sinks is the washing out of downstream jets due to the flow within the channel, which is deleterious to the stagnation heat transfer zone of the impinging jets.

Additive manufacturing (AM) has helped to fabricate and embody these hybrid heat sink approaches and other thermal management solutions having complex geometries. One recent example is an air jet cooler which leverages the design flexibility associated with 3D-printing to fabricate a customizable solution for cooling high power transistors with different package configurations [25]. They further managed to achieve convective heat transfer coefficients of up to 17 kW/m2K using air. Another water-based hybrid heat sink employed fractal channel manifolds to distribute the inlet water and deliver it to arrays of impinging jets [26].

Previously, the current authors proposed a hybrid microjet–microchannel water-cooled heat sink design concept (FINJET) that leveraged the design freedom afforded by the MICA-Freeform metal additive manufacturing process [27]. As will be discussed in more detail, the composite metal heat sink injects the fluid microjets into individual cells and subsequently exhausts the fluid into a microchannel sandwiched between the base and jet orifice plate. In [27] the design concept was shown to be feasible using CFD, with an effective thermal conductance of circa 400 kW/m2K achieved for a pressure drop of ~100 kPa. Of importance here is that the design is not only very high performance, but it is feasible for manufacture using an established high volume metal additive manufacturing technique; moreover, it has the potential to be at a much higher technology-readiness level compared with other concepts which rely on manufacturing expensive and labor-intensive techniques such as lithography, deep reactive ion etching, laser milling, diffusion bonding, etc. to fabricate the heat sinks.

The work here describes the manufacture and experimental characterization of the hybrid microjet–microchannel heat sink simulated in [27]. The overarching objective is to provide measurements which bring the simulated conceptual design to reality, while at the same time highlighting the challenges and achievements associated with manufacturing and characterizing millimeter-sized heat exchangers for extreme (~1000 W/cm2) heat flux levels. In order to demonstrate performance levels, a straight microchannel heat sink of identical footprint was manufactured and tested. This microchannel heat sink is posed here as the baseline to which the thermal–hydraulic performance of the FINJET heat sink is compared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and manufacturing of additively manufactured heat sinks
ce:section.ce:para: The FINJET design is fully described in a previous study by the authors [27] and for conciseness only the salient features will be discussed here.

The cooling technology developed here targets applications for millimeter-sized electronic components that generate heat fluxes in the region of 1000 W/cm2. This small size and extreme heat flux poses a significant challenge in terms of designing a heat exchanger not only of commensurate size of the device being cooled to facilitate high integration, but also using a practical manufacturing technique amenable to high quality and volume production. In this work, MICA Freeform, a proprietary metal additive manufacturing process established by Microfabrica Inc. was used. MICA Freeform is a layer-by-layer electrodeposition process whereby—akin to semiconductor electronics fabrication—each metal layer is deposited onto photoresist with patterned apertures. Subsequent removal of the photoresist and planarization results in a flat, planar, and geometrically featured metal layer of precisely controlled thickness onto which the next layer, with potentially different geometric patterns, is deposited. In such a way, this vertical, layer-by-layer build process is repeated, resulting in a 3D metal structure. Because the dimensional tolerances are held to ± 2 µm, intricate micron-sized structures can be fabricated. The MICA Freeform process provides minimum features as small as 20 µm in the horizontal plane and 5 µm along the vertical plane. A further advantage of this manufacturing technology is that multiple metals can be combined to produce composite materials that take advantage of their different mechanical and/or thermal properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hybrid impinging micro-jet array microchannel heat sink (FINJET)
ce:section.ce:para:
A sectional view of the FINJET architecture is shown in Figs. 1 and 2
. All surfaces in contact with water in the device are constructed from VALLOY-120 (k = 91 W/mK) due to its superior erosion and corrosion resistance, though other metals are also used in its construction, and these will be discussed in turn. As depicted, the overall vertical size is ~1 mm, which for a stand-alone heat exchanger can be considered very small in the context of what is available commercially and in terms of other concepts discussed in the literature. This height constraint was self-imposed because it conforms to the notion of being of commensurate size with the underlying electronic component being cooled.

As the figure shows, fluid enters an inlet chamber above the array of holes which has sufficient volume to act as a plenum, ostensibly stagnating the fluid. Fig. 2 illustrates that the pressurized fluid is then forced downward through 200 μm length and 70 μm width slot-type openings (Fig. 3 ) of vertical channels which then force the flow into individual cells. As Fig. 2 shows, the nozzle expels the fluid in such a way that it impinges on the base of the structure, while the confining walls of the cell force the fluid upward to a point where it is exhausted into a central microchannel that runs horizontally along the length of the unit.

With regard to the heat transfer, each cell is designed in such a way that the jet issues from the nozzle, creating a confined microjet against the lower heated surface, while the up-washing flow up the confining walls creates a microchannel flow between the 35 μm gap between the cell and the nozzle. Within the base and the cell walls, copper (k = 385 W/mK) is imbedded in order to improve heat conduction within the cell walls, which act as fins, as well as lateral heat spreading along the base. In this way, the individual cells create a high density of heat transfer area and the jet impingement and microchannel zones create regions of high convective heat transfer coefficient. Furthermore, the cellular architecture mitigates problems associated with sensible heating of the working fluid as well as any deleterious effect associated with washing-out of downstream jets by the bulk flow of spent fluid. As the simulation results of [27] show, this should result in exceptional temperature uniformity (±3 K from mean) even for a moderate flow rate of 0.5 L/min and imposed heat flux of 1000 W/cm2.

A novel feature of the FINJET design is the central microchannel. As mentioned, the spent flow from the jet cells is exhausted at the top of the cells into an interior 700 μm high microchannel that runs the 3 mm length of the unit. The jet nozzles protrude through the interior channel in such a way as to create offset strip fins within the channel, as depicted in the horizontal cross-sectional view of Fig. 3. Importantly, the nozzles are thermally coupled with the cell walls protruding from the heated base so that the nozzle housing is also heated in such a way that they act as heated fins within the internal microchannel. In this way, the nozzles serve the dual purpose of channeling the flow into the jet cells as well as adding heat transfer area as they guide the flow in the internal microchannel.

Fig. 4 shows a cross section of the entire lower jet-channel section. The overall dimensions are 10.65 mm X 11.03 mm X 0.97 mm with a 3.1 mm X 4.2 mm contact (heated) area on the lower base of the unit.

A plenum or header that is machined from a traditional subtractive process is soldered to the top of this device to complete and seal the fluid network. The device is enlarged at the top surface to facilitate soldering and to ensure a leak-proof solder joint. Additional solder corrals are added around the perimeter of the device fluid inlets and exits to prevent solder from flowing into the microjets and microchannels.

A microchannel heat sink with the same overall dimensions and contact area footprint was also fabricated using the same materials and process in order to establish a comparison with conventionally investigated microchannel heat sinks. A cross-sectional view of this baseline microchannel heat sink is shown in Fig. 5 . The heat sink portion consists of 11 channels with dimensions of 0.192 mm X 0.54 mm X 5.17 mm long (however only the first 3.1 mm are over the heated contact area, as shown in Fig. 5.)

Both the FINJET and the baseline microchannel heat sink were soldered to a 20 mm X 42 mm X 6.35 mm copper header, as shown in Fig. 6 , which was used to facilitate fluidic connections and thermal–hydraulic instrumentation. The header includes inlet and outlet ports, as well ports on either side of the FINJET, to measure pressure drop through the device.

The experimental facility used to test the thermal–hydraulic performance of the heatsinks is depicted in Fig. 7 . The test setup is comprised of an instrumented flow loop for supplying the flow to the heat exchanger and monitoring the hydraulic performance together with an instrumented heat block for thermal characterization. Water is circulated through the heat exchanger and header assembly, shown in Fig. 7, using a Micropump model GB-P23-DEELE pump. The pressure drop across the heat sink was measured using an Omega PX409-100DWUV differential pressure transducer and the flow rate was monitored by an Omega axial paddle wheel turbine type flowmeter (model FTB601B-T). An adequate developing length of straight tubing prior to the flowmeter was installed to help ensure flowmeter measurement accuracy. A 5 µm water filter was installed as per flowmeter specifications and to minimize the potential for particles to be introduced into the heat exchangers themselves. The water temperature at both the inlet and outlet of the header were measured with calibrated 1.59 mm diameter sheathed T-type thermocouples inserted parallel to the flow near the header Swagelok fittings.

The heat sink was positioned atop a heater block which was fabricated from OFHC copper. A diagram of the heater block–heat sink assembly is shown in Fig. 8
. The heater block was pyramidal and consisted of a 20 mm X 20 mm base section into which four 3.2 mm diameter X 19.1 mm long cartridge heaters (Sun Electric Heaters, Salem, Massachusetts) were inserted. The upper section of the heater block was tapered in such a way as to form a 1.1 mm long upper neck section with a cross section of 3.1 mm X 4.2 mm to match the contact area of the heat exchangers. The pyramidal shape of the heater block allowed for a high heat flux to be applied at the relatively small contact area of the heat sinks using commercially available cartridge heaters.

A 0.08 mm thick tin foil was used as thermal interface material (TIM) between the heater block neck and the heat sink base. The junction of a 0.076 mm diameter bare T-type thermocouple was centrally located on the heat sink base and sandwiched against the heat sink base to quantify Ts .
All measurements were logged using dedicated computer-controlled data acquisition hardware and software.

The hydraulic performance of the heat exchanger is related to the pressure drop between the inlet and outlet, as well as the hydraulic power requirement. Respectively, these are given as,
(1)
Δ
P =
P
i n
−
P
o u t
and
(2)
Q
p u m p
=
Δ
P
V ˙
where
V ˙
is the measured volumetric flow rate.

The primary thermal performance indicator for the heat sinks tested here is the effective thermal conductance, here defined as h, which is closely related to the convective heat transfer coefficient; however, here it accounts for the convective heat transfer over the extended surface structures within the device;
(3)
h =
q
″
b a s e
Δ
T
L M
where q// base is the applied heat flux at the bottom surface and ΔTLM is the log-mean temperature difference of the heat sink defined as
(4)
Δ
T w
=
Δ
T
L M
=
(
T s
−
T
w , o u t
)
−
(
T s

w , i n
)
In
[

T s

T
w , o u t
)
/
(
T s
−
T
w , i n
)
]
where Ts is the base surface temperature, Tw,in is the water inlet temperature, and Tw,out is the water outlet temperature. In this way, the effective thermal conductance reflects the overall thermal conductance of the heat sink, including conjugate heat transfer in the solid and convective heat transfer between the solid and fluid.

Correct estimation of the effective thermal conductance demands accurate estimation of the heat flux into the heat sink base and the average base surface temperature, Ts . For the former, the heat transfer to the water was calculated based on the measured temperature rise of the water between the inlet and outlet. Because we wish to quantify the heat flow into the base of the heat sink, we should subtract the relatively minor viscous heating effect caused by the pressure drop through the device (given by Eq. (2)) as follows:
(5)
Q
b a s e
=
Q w
−
Q
p u m p
= ρ
V ˙
C p
(
T
w , o u t
−
T
w , i n
)
−
Δ
P
V ˙

The small size of the heat sinks tested here makes direct measurement of the base temperature challenging. Furthermore, extrapolating the base temperature from the temperature gradient in the neck of the meter bar is not feasible as a result of the large temperature drop across the TIM which is required between the meter bar and the heat sink. As depicted in Fig. 8, a thin (85 µm) tin foil was sandwiched between the base of the heat sink and the meter bar to serve as a TIM. Taking advantage of the high thermal conductivity and malleability of tin, a bare thermocouple with 76 µm wire size was in contact with the base of the heat sinks and squeezed within the TIM in order to give an approximate heat sink base temperature. However, due to the extremely high heat fluxes in the tip of the heater block, there is a large temperature gradient across both the TIM and across the diameter of the embedded thermocouple (which is also partially in contact with the base of the heatsinks). Thus, the measured temperature is expected to be somewhat higher than the actual temperature of the heatsink bases such that the measurements would underestimate h. This also highlights the inherent difficulty in heat transfer measurements for very compact and very high heat flux heat exchangers. To provide a first approximation of the true h, the base surface temperature measurement, Ts , was adjusted by using the known temperature gradient to account for temperature drop across the thermocouple junction diameter in order to calculate a more representative temperature of the heat sink base. The temperature drop across this interface was estimated analytically using its thickness, the thermal conductivity of the tin foil, and the measured heat flux. The base temperature correction depended on the heat flux and ranged from 4 to 7 K.

A final performance indicator is the coefficient of performance (COP), which is the ratio of the thermal power transported through the heat exchanger and the hydraulic power required to force the liquid through it,
(6)
C O P =
Q
b a s e
Q
p u m p
where Qbase=q// baseAs and As is the surface area of the base of the heat sink.

A rigorous uncertainty analysis was employed to characterize the experimental uncertainty in all measured and calculated quantities. The uncertainties of the measured quantities are listed in Table 1 . The measured uncertainties were propagated to the uncertainties of the calculated quantities using the method by Kline and McClintock [28]. Here, each measured quantity is denoted by xi and the uncertainty in the measurement as Ui . The result of a calculations using these measurements is denoted Z, and the uncertainty in the calculated result is denoted by Uz . The uncertainty, Uz , is then calculated as
(7)
U z
=
(
∑
i = 1
n
[
∂ Z
∂
x i
U i
]
2
)
The uncertainty of the calculated quantities vary depending on the testing parameters and as such are plotted graphically using error bars.

To ensure the efficacy of thermal power calculation, an energy balance was measured between the electrical power to the heaters, as determined by the product of the applied voltage and current, and the power calculated using the water temperature rise calculated using Eq. (5) and is shown in Figure 9. Even with no insulation or guard heating, agreement was within 11% because the thermal resistance of the heat sinks is extremely low compared with the convective and radiative losses from the heater block to the ambient. Additional insulation or guard heating could serve to improve this energy balance, particularly at higher powers when the heater block temperature is high. However, for this study some heat loss can be tolerated because the heat flux was calculated using the water temperature rise (Eq. (5)).

As presented in [27], simulation-driven design was utilized to carry out an iterative design approach to reach the thermal–hydraulic performance targets detailed in [27]. Subsequent to the design stage, a prototype FINJET heat exchanger was fabricated using the MICA Freeform process previously outlined. An optical microscope image of the heat exchanger, without the soldered cap shown in Fig. 6, is shown in Fig. 10 and illustrates the scale and resolution afforded by this manufacturing technique.

Focusing now on the experimental results, Fig. 11 shows the important thermal performance characteristics of the FINJET heat exchanger for an applied heat flux of 1000 W/cm2 and inlet water temperature of 20 C. The effective thermal conductance is shown to increase with increased flow rate approximately as h∝
V ˙
n
where n<1.0 which is a typical power law dependence for forced convective systems. Importantly, the effective thermal conductance is measured to be h = 330 kW/m2K at a flow rate of 0.5 L/min. Related to this is the observation that the base surface temperature decreases with increased flow rate due to the decreasing thermal resistance at the higher flow rates. The specific thermal resistance or R-value (inverse of the thermal conductance) and thermal resistance are shown in Fig. 14 . The thermal resistance decreases with flow rate and is 0.23 K/W at 0.5 L/min, which is impressively small for a 4.1 mm X 3.2 mm heat sink. At 0.5 L/min and 1000 W/cm2, Fig. 11 shows that the base temperature rise over the water inlet temperature is ~32 C with an associated base temperature 52 C, which is notably lower than the maximum operating temperature of semiconductor electronics and provides sufficient headroom for situations which involve a higher inlet water temperature and/or allows for the portion of the thermal budget required for TIMs. This result, coupled with the predicted temperature homogeneity of the base (Ref [27]), could have a very positive impact with regard to device performance and reliability.

The hydraulic performance of the manufactured and tested FINJET heat exchanger is outlined in Fig. 12. Both the pumping power and pressure drop escalate in magnitude with increasing flow rate. The measured pressure drop is 160 kPa at the nominal flow rate of 0.5 L/min, though this is believed to be higher than what is achievable, considering the discussion above. Nonetheless it is of a magnitude which is not atypical for microfluidic heat exchangers. What is also of significance is the low pumping power requirement, as shown in Fig. 12. For 0.5 L/min, the pumping power is only 1.3 W, which results in a COP~100 for a heat flux of 1000 W/cm2 (Fig. 15 ), and this improves considerably with decreasing flow rate.

In the context of liquid cooled micro heat exchangers, microchannels are likely the most extensively researched technology based on the abundance of review articles over the last two decades. The main feature that makes microchannels so attractive is the fact that the convective heat transfer coefficient scales as the inverse of the channel hydraulic diameter. This, coupled with the high wetted surface area per unit volume, affords much higher effective thermal conductance than meso‑ or macro-scale heat exchangers. Other advantages, such as compactness and possible imbedding directly into semiconductor dies, have combined to drive intense interest in microchannels to the point that they can now be considered the de facto micro cooling strategy with which other concepts should be compared.

With this in mind, a baseline microchannel heat sink, with the same size and contact area footprint, was also manufactured using the same fabrication approach as discussed previously and shown in Fig. 5.

Fig. 16 shows the effective thermal conductance of the microjet and microchannel heat sinks. For these tests, the heat flux was adjusted at each test point so that the nominal base temperature was 60 C. This was deemed the most appropriate like-for-like test scenario because the microchannel could not support as high levels of heat flux while remaining in the single-phase flow regime. The controlled base temperature also provides a consistent means to compare the pressure drop because there is no major variation in the liquid viscosity between tests.

It is apparent from Fig. 16 that the FINJET system has significantly higher thermal conductance than the microchannel heat sink, though the microchannel can be considered to have quite high performance as well, achieving ~60 kW/m2 at 0.5 L/min. It is also clear that the microchannel shows very little dependence on the volumetric flow rate compared with the FINJET system. This is expected because the flow in the microchannel is laminar and thus is largely independent of flow velocity, with the slight increase likely due to developing region influences. In short, the very high convective heat transfer coefficients afforded by the impinging microjets, together with the higher wetted surface area within the FINJET, result in it significantly outperforming the microchannel heat sink with regard to thermal conductance, with the improvement increasing with flow rate. It must be noted that the numerical simulations show that there is a very minor base temperature variation for the microjet system, even at very high heat fluxes [27]. Although not measured or simulated here, the singular direction of the flow and resulting sensible heating of the liquid in the microchannels would cause base temperature variations between the inlet and outlet, and this would increase proportionately with the imposed heat flux, which is not desired in electronics cooling applications.

The hydraulic penalties associated with pressure drop and pumping power are illustrated in Fig. 17 . Due to the shallowness of the slope of the characteristic curves, the microjet and microchannel heat sinks behave similarly below 0.3 L/min, after which they diverge. However, it is once again noted that the solder flux residue has likely caused the pressure drop of the microjet system to be somewhat overstated compared with what can be ideally achieved, which is ~100 kPa at 0.5 L/min and is close to that measured for the microchannel.

A straightforward thermal–hydraulic performance comparison between the two microscale heat exchanger concepts is posed here by considering the ratios of the thermal conductance and the pressure drop, h/ΔP. The rationale is to consider a simple metric that quantifies the desired outcome versus the required penalty. In comparing the microjet and microchannel systems, the enhancement ratio is then simply defined as (h/ΔP)jets/(h/ΔP)channels . This is plotted in Fig. 18 and shows that (h/ΔP)jets remains substantially higher than (h/ΔP)channels , despite the fact that the latter has a lower pressure drop. In fact, the escalating gap between the pressure drops is offset by the escalating microjet heat sink thermal conductance in such a way that the enhancement ratio remains fairly constant, at about four-fold, for the range of flow rates tested.

A millimeter-sized liquid cooled heat exchanger has been fabricated using the precision MICA Freeform metal additive manufacturing process. The manufactured heat exchanger, with a heated target size of 4.1 mm X 3.2 mm, was tested. The heat exchanger consisted of an array of microjets that impinged on the heated base into individual micro-cells with walls designed to contain and redirect the flow while doubling as fin structures to increase the effective heat transfer area near the base. The flow exits the cells into a centrally located transverse microchannel where the nozzles, which are thermally coupled to the heated base, create an offset strip fin heat exchanger architecture. In this way, the flow is conditioned so that the well-known benefits of microfluidic heat transfer are coupled with a very high heat transfer surface area per unit volume. The tested FINJET heat exchanger compared favorably with simulation results published in [27]. To the extent of our knowledge, the FINJET design has produced one of the highest reported single-phase effective thermal conductances, achieving 330 kW/m2K at a flow rate of 0.5 L/min and a required pressure drop of 160 kPa. Other important performance metrics include:
•
pumping power of 1.3 W at 0.5 L/min,
•
COP of circa 100 at 0.5 L/min,
•
a near-constant enhancement ratio of 6 compared with a microchannel heat exchanger of commensurate design, and
•
maximum measured thermal conductance of approximately 350 kW/m2K at 0.6 L/min.

Future work will involve refining the assembly process of the heat exchanger in order to target reduction in the pressure drop without compromising the heat transfer effectiveness. Also, work will be performed to develop this heat exchanger for larger surface area applications and integration into an all-in-one cooling loop for real-life electronics cooling applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
